title: Calculus & gradients + DSA Bit Manipulation kickoff
focus: Chain rule, partial derivatives, Jacobians, Hessians — what backprop will use.

items.label: Single Number
interviewQuestions: Why does XOR-all give the unique element when others appear twice?
How does this generalize when others appear three times (LC 137)?

items.label: Number OF 1 Bits
interviewQuestions: Walk me through the bit trick used here, bit by bit on a small input.
Why XOR / AND / shift specifically — what property of that operation does the problem exploit?
What's the complexity in terms of bits (often O(32) → O(1)), and where could that break for big-int?

items.label: Counting Bits
interviewQuestions: Walk me through the bit trick used here, bit by bit on a small input.
Why XOR / AND / shift specifically — what property of that operation does the problem exploit?
What's the complexity in terms of bits (often O(32) → O(1)), and where could that break for big-int?

items.label: Derivatives, partial derivatives, chain rule
interviewQuestions: Derive the chain rule for f(g(x)) and apply it to a 2-layer neural network.
What's the difference between a partial derivative and a directional derivative?

items.label: Gradients, Jacobians, Hessians
interviewQuestions: What does the gradient vector represent geometrically?
When would you need the full Jacobian or Hessian in ML?
Why do second-order methods (Newton) rarely scale to deep nets?

items.label: Backprop as the chain rule on a computation graph
interviewQuestions: Explain backpropagation as the chain rule applied to a computation graph.
Why do vanishing gradients happen, and how do ReLU / residual connections help?
